nodes.title: Build a cache key from arguments
nodes.why: We need one stable key per argument list so repeated calls can find the same cached result.
nodes.alternative: Use only the first argument as the key.
nodes.tradeoff: JSON keys are easy, but they are limited for circular or non-serializable values.

nodes.title: Return fast when value is already cached
nodes.why: This is the core speedup: skip recomputation and reuse the previous result.
nodes.alternative: Always recompute and update cache every time.
nodes.tradeoff: Very fast on repeats, but cache can keep growing if inputs keep changing.

nodes.title: Save computed result for next call
nodes.why: After the first calculation, future calls with the same key can return instantly.
nodes.alternative: Store only selected keys with a custom policy.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple and effective, but you may eventually want eviction for long-lived usage.

nodes.title: Walk one map level per argument
nodes.why: Each argument moves you deeper into a path, so argument tuples get separate cache branches.
nodes.alternative: Flatten arguments into one JSON string key.
nodes.tradeoff: Works with object identity well, but the structure is more complex than a flat map.

nodes.title: Check terminal sentinel for cached result
nodes.why: `'result'` marks the final value at that argument path, so we can return immediately on hits.
nodes.alternative: Store a small object like `{ value, hasValue }` at each node.
nodes.tradeoff: Sentinel is lightweight, but it relies on a reserved key convention.

nodes.title: Write computed value at the leaf node
nodes.why: This stores the result exactly where that argument chain ends, ready for future lookups.
nodes.alternative: Keep a separate map from path ids to results.
nodes.tradeoff: Direct leaf storage is straightforward, but map trees can use more memory for many unique paths.
